To address these challenges, the QUIC (Quick UDP
Internet Connections) protocol,  initially developed by
Google and standardized in RFC 9000 [4], has emerged
as a promising alternative. QUIC leverages UDP as its
transport layer, enabling a 0-RTT handshake for known
servers,  integrating TLS 1.3 for enhanced security [5],
and supporting stream multiplexing to mitigate HOL
blocking.  These  features make QUIC particularly
suitable for modern web applications such as HTTP/3,
where low latency and high security are critical.
Despite QUIC’s advantages,  its adoption  in tra-

ditional TCP-based applications remains  limited [6].
Many legacy systems and applications rely heavily on
TCP’s ordered delivery and reliability guarantees. This
dichotomy raises the question:  how can we harness
QUIC’s benefits while maintaining compatibility with
TCP-based applications? One potential solution is to
encapsulate TCP traffic within QUIC tunnels, leverag-
ing QUIC’s features while preserving the familiar se-
mantics of TCP.
This paper explores the  implementation and per-
formance evaluation of a TCP over QUIC tunneling
scheme.  By encapsulating TCP traffic within QUIC
packets, we aim to bridge the gap between traditional
TCP applications and modern QUIC-based infrastruc-
ture.  The study focuses on key performance metrics
such as latency, throughput, and reliability across di-
verse network conditions. Through experimental eval-
uation, we demonstrate QUIC’s superiority in high-loss
and fluctuating network environments, while also ad-
dressing challenges such as compatibility issues, com-
putational overhead, and security concerns.
The remainder of this paper is structured as follows.
Section 2 provides a detailed comparison of QUIC
and TCP core mechanisms, along with an overview of
network tunneling principles. Section 3 reviews related
work on QUIC and TCP performance studies, as well
as network tunneling research.  Section 4 describes
,  Vol. ??,  No. ??,  ????

2.1.  Comparison  of  QUIC and TCP core
mechanisms
The Transmission Control Protocol (TCP), as defined
in RFC 9293 [1] and RFC 793 [2], has been fundamental
for reliable Internet data transmission. However, QUIC,
initially developed by Google and standardized  in
RFC 9000 [4], addresses TCP’s limitations, particularly
for web applications requiring low latency and high
security. A detailed comparison reveals:
•  Connection Establishment:  TCP employs a
three-way handshake  (SYN,  SYN-ACK, ACK),
requiring at least one RTT, with additional RTT
for TLS 1.3 negotiation, as noted in Performance
evaluation in TCP and QUIC [7].  QUIC uses
UDP as its transport layer, which enables a 0-RTT
handshake for known servers. By merging the TCP
and TLS handshakes  into a single three-packet
exchange, QUIC reduces  latency and enhances
initial data transmission speed.
•  Multiplexing and Streams: TCP operates as
a single ordered byte stream, susceptible to head-
of-line (HOL) blocking, where packet loss delays
all subsequent data.  QUIC, as detailed in RFC
9000, supports arbitrary concurrent streams within
a connection, each with independent flow control,
mitigating HOL blocking and improving resource
utilization [8].
•  Security: TCP relies on separate TLS implemen-
tation post-handshake (RFC 8446), adding over-
head.  QUIC integrates TLS 1.3,  encrypting all
packets by default, reducing handshake latency and
enhancing privacy, as discussed in QUIC vs TCP
computational efficiency [9].
•  Congestion Control: Both protocols implement
congestion control, with TCP using algorithms like
New Reno (RFC 6582) [10] and QUIC adopting
similar mechanisms (RFC 9002)  [11].  However,
QUIC’s  user-space  implementation  allows  for
more agile updates,  potentially offering better
The Computer Journal

Bao and Ying Chen
adaptability to network conditions.
•  Transport  Layer  Implementation:  TCP
benefits  from kernel-level  optimizations,  reusing
state  for efficiency.  QUIC,  running over UDP
in user mode,  sacrifices  some kernel  efficiency
for  flexibility,  as  highlighted  in  performance
evaluations  showing higher per-packet overhead
due to user-kernel boundary crossings [9].
2.2.  Network tunneling principle
Network tunneling, involves encapsulating one protocol
within another to transport data across incompatible
networks or enhance security [12], commonly used in
virtual private networks (VPNs). For TCP over QUIC
tunnels, the process entails wrapping TCP traffic within
QUIC packets, leveraging QUIC’s features like reduced
latency and integrated security.
Two modes are relevant [13]:
•  Datagram Mode:  This mode, as proposed in
Tunneling TCP inside QUIC [14],  encapsulates
entire packets,  including network and transport
headers, within QUIC datagrams.  It  supports
any network-layer protocol but incurs significant
overhead, with each packet requiring additional
IP/UDP/QUIC headers, making it inefficient for
TCP, especially with small packets.
•  StreamMode: Detailed in Tunneling TCP inside
QUIC [14], this mode transports TCP bytestreams
over QUIC streams, reducing overhead by eliminat-
ing redundant headers. It uses Type-Length-Value
(TLV) messages for negotiation, such as TCP Con-
nect TLV (0x00, 20 bytes) for destination specifi-
cation, and supports flow control tuned to TCP’s
receive window, enhancing efficiency for applica-
tions with frequent small packets. For this reason,
we will adopt the stream mode in the implementa-
tion.

3.1.  QUIC and TCP Performance Studies
The comparative performance of QUIC and TCP has
been extensively studied in recent years, particularly in
the context of web and mobile applications requiring
low latency, high throughput,  and strong reliability.
QUIC, as a user-space transport protocol built atop
UDP,  introduces  innovations  such  as multiplexed
streams,  integrated TLS 1.3, and 0-RTT connection
establishment, positioning it as a successor to TCP for
many latency-sensitive applications.
Several  studies have also focused on the efficiency
of QUIC implementations in practice. They compared
the CPU utilization of QUIC and TCP during high-
load transmission, showing that QUIC consumes more
computational resources due to user-space processing
and encryption overhead.  Nevertheless,  this  trade-
off is often justified by QUIC’s resilience in lossy or
,  Vol. ??,  No. ??,  ????

Implementation and Performance Eva
fluctuating networks, where TCP performance degrades
rapidly.
In the  context  of  tunneling,  encapsulating TCP
traffic  over QUIC offers  a  hybrid  solution  that
combines TCP’s widespread application compatibility
with QUIC’s  transport-layer  robustness.  This has
motivated  emerging  research  into TCP-over-QUIC
systems that seek to leverage QUIC’s  loss recovery,
multiplexing, and security capabilities without altering
the  semantics  of  existing TCP applications.  Our
study extends this  line of  research by implementing
and experimentally evaluating a practical TCP-over-
QUIC tunnel using the Quinn library in Rust, further
validating the protocol’s strengths and trade-offs under
controlled packet loss and latency conditions.
3.2.  Comparison of different QUIC implemen-
tations
Comparison of different QUIC implementations reveals
notable differences in performance and characteristics.
According to the work by Salim Hertelli et al.  [15],
various QUIC implementations such as Chromium-quic
”Quiche”, Cloudflare ”Quiche”, Mvfst, Msquic, Quic-
go, Aioquic,  and Haskell-quic have been developed
in diverse programming languages like C/C++, Rust,
Go,  Python,  and Haskell.  These  implementations
support different versions of QUIC and employ various
congestion  control  algorithms,  including  CUBIC,
NewReno, and HyStart++. While all adhere to IETF
specifications,  they exhibit  significant variations  in
project status, maintenance,  supported features, and
performance metrics.
In a more detailed benchmarking study by Kevin
Marti  [16], MsQuic and Quinn were compared under
different networking conditions. The results indicated
that  increasing  the  segment  size  for Quinn  can
significantly enhance its performance, up to doubling
it in some cases.  This suggests that Quinn benefits
from larger  segment  sizes,  which  can  reduce  the
overhead associated with frequent  system calls and
data  copying.  In  fact,  the  patched  version  of
Quinn, which sends  larger  segments,  demonstrated
improved throughput and fewer system calls compared
to its unpatched version.  This highlights a potential
advantage of Quinn in scenarios where segment size can
be optimized. However, when packet loss or reordering
was  introduced, MsQuic  consistently outperformed
Quinn by at least 50%.  This indicates that MsQuic
has more  robust mechanisms  for handling network
instability.  Despite this, Quinn’s ability to achieve
high performance with optimized segment sizes and
reduced system calls suggests that  it can be highly
efficient under stable network conditions. Overall, while
MsQuic demonstrates  superior  resilience to network
instability, Quinn’s performance can be significantly
enhanced through specific optimizations, highlighting
the  importance of  implementation-specific tuning  in
The Computer Journal

luation of TCP over QUIC Tunnels  3
QUIC deployments.  Therefore, we will use Quinn’s
QUIC implementation to complete the TCP over QUIC
tunnel construction.
3.3.  Identified Deficiencies
Currently,  there  is  no mature  or  standardized
implementation of TCP over QUIC written in Rust.
While  some  experimental  projects  exist,  they are
often incomplete or tailored to narrow use cases.  In
this work, rather than aiming for a production-ready
or  fully optimized solution,  we present  a minimal
and functional  implementation intended primarily for
empirical  evaluation.  The  goal  is  to provide  a
foundation for systematic performance testing and to
better understand the behavior and trade-offs of TCP
over QUIC tunneling under various network conditions.
Moreover, there is a noticeable lack of comprehensive
and methodologically sound performance evaluations
for TCP over QUIC tunnel architectures. Prior studies
have  largely focused on comparing QUIC and TCP
as standalone transport protocols, without exploring
the unique characteristics and trade-offs introduced by
encapsulating TCP over QUIC. In particular, metrics
such as CPU overhead,  throughput under varying
packet  loss  rates,  and performance  in high-latency
environments have not been systematically addressed
in the context of tunneling.
This paper aims to fill these gaps by implementing
a practical TCP over QUIC tunnel  in Rust  and
conducting targeted performance evaluations across a
range of network conditions.
4.  SYSTEM DESIGN &  IMPLEMENTA-
TION
This section delineates the implementation details of
the TCP over QUIC tunneling.  The Figure 1 shows
the data flow through the tunnel. The implementation
comprises three core components:
•  A data transfer utility facilitating bidirectional
communication
•  A server-side QUIC tunnel endpoint
•  A client-side TCP to QUIC proxy
The system was implemented in Rust leveraging the
Tokio [17] asynchronous runtime and Quinn [18] QUIC
library.
4.1.

Bidirectional Data Transfer Mechanism
The core data transfer functionality is encapsulated in
the bidirectional copy utility function.  This asyn-
chronous function establishes a full-duplex communica-
tion channel between a TCP stream and a QUIC con-
nection by:
1.  Splitting the TCP stream into separate reader and
writer using tokio::net::TcpStream::split()
,  Vol. ??,  No. ??,  ????

4  Xuanhong Guo, Zekun
FIGURE 1. Improved Principle of QUIC-Tun: Showing the fl
tunnel to a remote TCP server.
2.  Creating two parallel asynchronous tasks
•  Continuously copies data from TCP to QUIC
send stream until EOF
•  Relays  incoming QUIC data  to  the TCP
writer
3.  Employing tokio::try join! macro to synchro-
nize both tasks while propagating any errors
4.  Implementing proper stream shutdown procedures
upon completion or failure
The  implementation  ensures  atomic  operation
through Rust’s ownership system and automatic re-
source management.
4.2.  Server-Side
The QUIC server implementation operates as a persis-
tent tunnel endpoint with the following architectural
characteristics:
4.2.1.  Connection Handling
•  Utilizes Quinn’s Endpoint abstraction to listen for
incoming QUIC connections
•  Spawns independent Tokio tasks for each connec-
tion to ensure isolation
•  Implements continuous stream acceptance through
accept bi() for bidirectional communication
4.2.2.  Configuration Parameters
•  Generates self-signed certificates for development
purposes via rcgen.
•  Configures transport parameters including:
–  Maximum  100  concurrent  bidirectional
streams
–  Disabled unidirectional streams
–  2-second keep-alive interval
•  Maintains compatibility with standard TLS certifi-
cate verification.
4.2.3.  Tunnel Establishment
•  For each accepted QUIC stream, establishes a new
TCP connection to the target service
The Computer Journal

Bao and Ying Chen
ow of network traffic from a local TCP client through a QUIC
•  Delegate data transfer to the bidirectional copy
utility
•  Implements robust error handling for connection
timeouts and application-level closures
4.3.  Client-Side
The client component functions as a TCP to QUIC
proxy with the following implementation details:
4.3.1.  Connection Initiation
•  Binds  to  specified  local  TCP  port  using
tokio::net::TcpListener
•  Establish QUIC connection to remote endpoint up-
on TCP client connection
•  Opens new bidirectional QUIC stream for each
TCP connection
4.3.2.  Security Configuration
•  Supports configurable certificate verification:
–  Standard TLS certificate chain validation
–  Insecure mode with disabled server verifica-
tion (for testing)
•  Maintains  identical  keep-alive  parameters  (2-
second interval) with server
4.3.3.  Data Forwarding
•  Instantiates separate Tokio task per connection
•  Utilizes same bidirectional copy mechanism for
consistent data transfer behavior
•  Implements comprehensive error logging for oper-
ation monitoring
4.4.  System Integration
The main application provides  a unified  interface
through CLI commands with Clap [19] crate:
•  Server  mode:  quic-tun serve --dest <TCP
SocketAddr> --bind <QUIC SocketAddr>
•  Client mode:  quic-tun connect --dest <QUIC
SocketAddr> --bind <TCP SocketAddr>

This  section presents a comprehensive experimental
methodology to evaluate the performance characteris-
tics of the TCP over QUIC tunneling implementation.
5.1.  Experimental Setup
All experiments were conducted in a controlled virtual
network  environment  to  ensure  repeatability  and
minimal external interference. The setup included the
following components:
•  Virtual Machines: Two Debian 12.10.0 virtual
machines deployed on  the  same physical  host
using KVM/QEMU virtualization. Each VM was
configured with:
–  4 vCPUs (with host-passthrough mode en-
abled for precise timing)
–  8 GB RAM
•  Operating System and Kernel:
–  Debian GNU/Linux 12.10.0 (Bookworm)
–  Linux Kernel version 6.1 (default)
•  Unit Definitions:
–  Bandwidth: Measured in MB/s (megabytes
per second)
–  Throughput: Measured in Mbps (megabits
per second).  Conversions follow 1 MB/s =
8 Mbps
•  Protocol Implementations:
–  TCP: Default Linux TCP stack using Cubic
congestion control
–  QUIC:  Implemented via the Quinn library
(Rust)
•  Performance Measurement Tool: iperf3
–  iperf3 server was deployed on one VM and
the client on the other
–  Tests were repeated 5 times for each protocol
to obtain average values
–  Each test uses the default iperf3 parameter
–  The measured metrics included:
∗  Throughput (in Mbps)
∗  CPU utilization during transmission
•  Extra System Monitoring:
The Computer Journal

luation of TCP over QUIC Tunnels  5
–  tcpdump was used to capture traffic for post-
analysis and validation
5.2.  Evaluation Metrics
The experimental framework measures:
•  Throughput:
D
Throughput =
t
Where:
–  D is the amount of data transferred (in bits)
–  t is the transfer time (in seconds)
•  CPU Usage:
C × P
CPU Usage =  × 100%
T
where:
–  C: CPU cycles required to process one packet
–  P : Number of packets processed per second
(packets/sec)
–  T :  Total CPU cycles available per second
(e.g., CPU frequency × number of cores)
When comparing tunneling technologies  to direct
connections,  it  is generally unnecessary to measure
RTT (Round-Trip Time)  in isolation.  Any latency
introduced by the tunnel will naturally manifest  in
end-to-end performance indicators such as throughput,
application-level  response time,  or CPU utilization.
Since RTT represents only one component of the overall
performance footprint,  separately testing it provides
limited diagnostic value unless  latency is a primary
concern.
Furthermore,  in scenarios where TCP traffic is en-
capsulated within a QUIC-based tunnel — despite be-
ing a non-standard configuration —RTT measurements
taken at the TCP layer become methodologically un-
sound.  This  is because such RTT values are  influ-
enced by QUIC’s internal mechanisms,  including con-
gestion control, retransmission logic, and multiplexing
of streams.  Consequently, the observed RTT reflects
a compound delay rather than the actual network-level
round-trip time, making it unreliable for precise latency
assessment or performance benchmarking.

We firstly did a  series  on the  effects  of  different
bandwidths on the two connections, as shown in Table
1:
It is not difficult to find that the throughput of TCP
direct connection can completely crush the TCP over
QUIC tunnel under excellent network conditions, but
the TCP over QUIC tunnel  itself  is to improve the
efficiency under weak network conditions,  so in the
following experiment, we use the bandwidth of 10MB/s
by default.

6.2.  Packet Loss Resilience
As illustrated in Table 2, We evaluated QUIC Tunnel
and direct TCP at different packet loss rates using the
default test parameters of iperf3. The packet loss rate
is formally defined as:
Nlost
Packet Loss Rate =  × 100%
Nsent
where Nlost  denotes  the number of  lost packets,
and Nsent  represents  the  total  number  of  packets
transmitted.  This metric provides  a  standardized
framework for assessing the system’s robustness in the
presence of unreliable network conditions.
FIGURE 2.  This figure shows the impact of  the two

The results reveal several key observations:
•  Expected Early TCP Advantage:  As  ex-
pected, plain TCP slightly outperforms the QUIC
tunnel at low packet loss rates (up to 5%) due to
the overhead introduced by encapsulating traffic
through dual protocol layers in the tunnel.
•  Consistent Throughput Advantage:  But,
Beyond  5% packet  loss,  the  QUIC  tunnel
consistently outperforms direct TCP, maintaining
throughput  from 58.3 Mbps  at  5%  loss  to
11.1 Mbps at 20% loss.
•  Graceful Performance Degradation:  QUIC
demonstrates more  graceful  degradation under
increasing packet loss, whereas TCP performance

Observations:
•  Throughput Decrease with Latency:  Both
protocols exhibit the expected inverse relationship
between  latency and throughput.  As  latency
increases,  throughput decreases  for both QUIC
Tunnel  and Plain TCP.

While QUIC Tunnel
maintains a higher throughput compared to Plain
TCP at  lower  latencies (e.g., 0 ms and 50 ms),
the performance gap narrows as latency increases.
At higher  latencies,  both protocols  experience
significant throughput degradation.
•  Relative Stability of QUIC Tunnel:  QUIC
Tunnel  demonstrates  relatively  more  stable
throughput at higher latencies.  For instance, at
1000 ms, the throughput of QUIC Tunnel  is still
notably higher (6.60 Mbps on the sender side, 2.76
Mbps on the receiver side) compared to Plain TCP
(0.73 Mbps and 0.61 Mbps,  respectively).  How-

•  Impact of Latency on Plain TCP: Plain TCP
shows a more pronounced decrease in throughput
as  latency  increases, with a particularly sharp
decline at  latencies of 500 ms and beyond.  In
contrast, QUIC Tunnel’s throughput degradation
is less severe under similar conditions,  indicating
that QUIC’s protocol design offers a degree of
resilience to latency.
6.4.  Packet Reordering Tolerance
The out-of-order rate is formally defined as:
Nout-of-order

The out-of-order delivery tests from Table 4 reveal:
And we can find that:
•  QUIC’s advantage in reordering: QUIC can
handle packet reordering more effectively due to
its stream-based, out-of-order tolerant design.
•  TCP  over  QUIC doesn’t  fully  inherit
QUIC’s advantages: Despite being encapsulated
in QUIC, TCP still relies on its in-order delivery
and retransmission mechanisms,  which can be
affected by reordering.

This paper presents the implementation and evaluation
of a TCP-based tunneling scheme over QUIC, with
the aim of  leveraging the advantages of QUIC while

maintaining compatibility with traditional TCP-based
applications. The results of comprehensive experiments
demonstrate that under lossy or high-latency network
conditions, TCP over QUIC offers significant resilience
and performance advantages compared to native TCP
connections.  These benefits are primarily attributed
to the  inherent  features of QUIC,  including stream
multiplexing and efficient loss recovery mechanisms.
However, our evaluation also reveals the limitations of
TCP over QUIC in high-quality network environments.
In such conditions, the dual-protocol stack introduces
additional  computational  overhead and  latency due
to user-space processing,  encryption,  and protocol
encapsulation. This overhead leads to lower throughput
and higher CPU usage compared to direct TCP, which
benefits from kernel-level optimizations. As such, while
TCP over QUIC is highly effective in degraded or mobile
network scenarios, it is less efficient in well-provisioned,
stable networks.
Further  research  could  explore more  forms  of
QUIC-based network tunneling,  such as TCP over
WebTransport [22], to further develop the potential of
QUIC in the transformation of the network protocol.
In addition, TCP over QUIC tunnel can optimize the
processing of different application layer protocols and
improve the performance of traditional application layer
protocols under this tunnel.
